notes: Test PC opened https://copilot.microsoft.com/ in guest mode (signed-out). Hard-reloaded, installed fetch+XHR trace, located #userInput textarea (id=userInput), set value via React-friendly setter, dispatched input/change events, then clicked the 'message submit' (메시지 제출) button — input cleared, user message bubble rendered in DOM (optimistic UI). Re-attempted with native CDP Input.insertText + Enter keyDown/keyUp. Polled DOM for 24s after retry, plus ~50s after first attempt. Result: NO /api/chat or /c/api/chat POST or WebSocket frame ever fired in either attempt — only the autosuggest GET (status 200, empty suggestions) and Microsoft OneCollector telemetry POSTs. Final screenshot reveals the cause: Copilot rendered a hCaptcha-style anti-bot challenge ('사람인지 확인하십시오' = 'Verify you are human') in the middle of the chat area with the typing-indicator dots stuck. This is a Microsoft client-side anti-automation gate, NOT the APF engine — the chat request was suppressed by CAPTCHA before any HTTPS request that could be MITM-intercepted by etap was emitted. Empirical signal about m365 iter2 deploy spillover into copilot is therefore unobtainable in this run: we cannot distinguish 'engine still doesn't fire on copilot' from 'engine never got a chance because Microsoft blocked the send client-side'. Helper-key probe found NO APF-injected globals (only copilotTrustedTypesPolicy, pidlResourceHelpers — both first-party Microsoft); fetch and XHR.prototype.open are native [native code]. m365_copilot path (BREAKTHROUGH OK at #986) is structurally different: m365 lives at m365.cloud.microsoft, uses signed-in account + signalR with handshake, while consumer copilot.microsoft.com uses copilot_chat_send envelope but is now CAPTCHA-gated for guest-mode automated submits. Recommend: (1) login to consumer copilot via user account before retry, OR (2) bypass CAPTCHA via human-in-the-loop solve, OR (3) accept that consumer copilot is structurally untestable from this automation harness in guest mode and downgrade priority. Prior copilot FAILs (#893/#896/#901) likely had the same root cause — they predate m365 iter2 deploy so the m365 fix cannot have changed copilot behavior here in either direction (no positive or negative spillover observable).
